sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 54-year-old male, presents with a complaint of stabbing, shooting pain in the right cheek bone area of his face, which has been present for approximately 2-3 months.
He describes the pain as severe, often reaching a level of 10 out of 10, and is triggered by certain temperatures and cold weather.
He has tried ibuprofen, Motrin, and Tiger Balm without relief.
He has a past medical history of occasional headaches, but no history of migraines or brain tumors.
He denies numbness or tingling in the affected area, but reports a little numbness on his right side when the doctor applies cotton tape.
Past medical history: The patient has a past medical history of occasional headaches, but no history of migraines, brain tumors, multiple sclerosis, or other neurological conditions.
Review of systems: The patient denies any other symptoms in other organs or systems.
Current medications: None mentioned.
Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient is able to follow the doctor's finger in both directions and shows symmetric teeth.
He can hear sounds on both sides of his face.
The doctor applies cotton tape and notes that the patient reports a little numbness on his right side.
The doctor then uses a needle to poke the patient's face and notes that the patient reports the left side as sharp and the right side as dull.
Test Results: MRI results show a small blood vessel abutting and perhaps pinching the trigeminal nerve.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient is experiencing symptoms consistent with trigeminal neuralgia, a condition where nerve compression causes shooting electrical pain in the face.
Problem list: Trigeminal neuralgia; active.
Plan:
I prescribed Tegretol 100mg to be taken twice daily for the management of trigeminal neuralgia.
I recommended that the patient follow up in one month to assess the efficacy of the medication.
The patient will undergo genetic testing to ensure the medication is safe for them.
Follow-up: The patient will return in one month to reassess the efficacy of the medication and discuss further treatment options if necessary.
